does: This position paper argues that NeurIPS should establish and enforce reproducibility standards for papers making frontier AI safety claims—assertions that capable models are safe, adequately mitigated, or suitable for deployment. The authors document an evidential inversion where the most consequential safety claims receive the least scrutiny because essential artifacts (model weights, test data, implementation details) are routinely withheld. They frame non-reproducibility not as a minor transparency issue but as a fundamental failure of evaluation methodology, citing evidence from the 2026 International AI Safety Report showing pre-deployment testing has become harder and models now distinguish test from deployment contexts, combined with transparency index data revealing inadequate disclosure of train-test overlap and measurement-theory work on attack-success-rate confounds.
connects: This work extends longstanding debates about reproducibility and transparency in machine learning by focusing specifically on high-stakes safety claims in frontier AI. It builds on prior transparency initiatives and indices that have documented the inadequacy of current disclosure practices, responds to concerns about model developers' ability to game safety evaluations, and engages with measurement-theory critiques showing how standard safety metrics can be gamed or misleading. The paper draws on governance and policy literature questioning how scientific claims should be validated before consequential decisions, while connecting to accountability frameworks and institutional-design arguments about how conference standards shape research norms—particularly work examining how publication venues enforce methodological rigor in empirical claims.
enables: This position paper could catalyze a shift in how the AI safety research community validates and publishes high-stakes safety findings, establishing reproducibility as a prerequisite rather than an aspirational norm. If adopted, such standards would enable more rigorous external auditing of frontier model claims, create pressure for greater artifact sharing, and potentially improve the evidential basis for deployment decisions. The work points toward future institutional mechanisms for safety validation—such as gated access to model weights for independent researchers, standardized evaluation protocols with disclosed test distributions, and clearer separation of pre-deployment testing from post-hoc published results. It also opens conversations about how conference governance itself can serve as a tool for improving scientific standards in high-stakes domains.
